title: Assertion arguments should be passed in the correct order
description: Assertion arguments should be passed in the correct orderAnalyze your codeintentionality-logicalmaintainabilityCode SmelltestssuspiciousWhy is this an issue?How can I fix it?The standard assertions library methods such asAreEqualandAreSameinMSTestandNUnit, orEqualandSameinXUnit, expect the first argument to be the expected value and the second argument to be the actual value.What is the potential impact?Having the expected value and the actual value in the wrong order will not alter the outcome of tests, (succeed/fail when it should) but the error messages will contain misleading information.This rule raises an issue when the actual argument to an assertions library method is a hard-coded value and the expected argument is not.Available In:Catch issues on the fly,in your IDEDetect issues i
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

noncompliant: Assert.AreEqual(actualValue, 5); // Noncompliant - arguments in wrong order
Assert.AreEqual(result, "expected"); // Noncompliant - actual first, expected second
compliant: Assert.AreEqual(5, actualValue); // Compliant - expected first, actual second

checks.message: Assert.AreEqual arguments should be in order: expected, actual.
